en.extract:
The African scops owl (Otus senegalensis) is a small owl which is widespread in sub-Saharan Africa.


Taxonomy
William Swainson first described the species in 1837 from a specimen collected in Senegal, and initially assigned it to the now defunct genus Scops. Today the species is assigned to the genus Otus.
There are three subspecies currently recognised:

O. s. senegalensis: Widespread in sub-Saharan Africa
O. s. nivosus: Found in south-eastern Kenya from the lower Tana River to the Lali Hills
O. s. feae: Endemic to Annobón island in the Gulf of Guinea (regarded by Birdlife International as a separate species, the Annobón scops owl)
The African scops owl was formerly regarded as the African subspecies of the European scops owl. It was grouped with the Arabian scops owl, Socotra scops owl, and the Annobón scops owl into O. senegalensis; however, these species are now considered to be separate.


Description
The African scops owl is a small owl, measuring 17 cm (6.7 in) in length. It is typically greyish-brown, though sometimes pale rufous or warmer brown, and is cryptically marked with streaks and mottling. Its grey facial disk has a narrow black edge, and its eyes are yellow. It has ear tufts, which are generally kept lowered unless the bird is disturbed. The African scops owl is around 15–17 cm (5.9–6.7 in) in length with a wingspan of 45 cm (18 in).


Voice
The African scops owl gives a monotonous quivering distinctive "prrrp" at five second intervals.


Similar species
The migrant Eurasian scops owl is very similar to the African scops owl; while it is typically slightly larger, it may not be distinguishable in the field.


Range and habitat
The African scops owl is endemic to sub-Saharan Africa. It ranges from sea level to 2,000 m (6,600 ft) in elevation, and is found in wooded habitats and forest edge, including in gardens and mangroves.


Behaviour
The African scops owl is strictly nocturnal. During the day, it perches close to the trunk of a tree. When roosting in daylight, this species closes its eyes and extends its ear-tufts to give the impression of a tree branch, making it easily overlooked. Pairs sometimes roost together. The African scops owl is not as territorial as the Eurasian scops owl and will nest in loose aggregations, with the nest sites relatively close to each other.
The male and female may duet, calling all night both before and after leaving the roost site. The African scops owl lays four to six eggs directly onto the floor of a tree hollow, with laying occurring throughout April and June. Incubation lasts about 27 days, during which the female incubates the eggs and is fed by the male. Once the eggs hatch the young are fed by the female with food brought by the male. The young fledge in 30 days.
The prey of the African scops owl is mainly insects such as grasshoppers, beetles, crickets, moths etc. but also spiders, scorpions and small vertebrates. They normally hunt by scanning or listening from a perch and swooping down to capture prey on the ground, but will hawk for flying insects.


References


External links

African Scops Owl - Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds.
African Scops-Owl (Otus senegalensis) videos and photos at Internet Bird Collection

National Reserve]], Kenya | taxon = Otus senegalensis | authority = ([[William Swainson|Swainson]], 1837) }} The '''African scops owl''' ('''''Otus senegalensis''''') is a small [[owl]] which is widespread in [[sub-Saharan]] [[Africa]]. [[File:Owl African Scops 2015 06 02 17 42 23 6156.jpg|right|thumb|An African scops owl in the [[Manyoni Private Game Reserve|Zululand Rhino Reserve]]]] ==Taxonomy== [[William Swainson]] first described the species in 1837 from a specimen collected in Senegal, and initially assigned it to the now defunct genus ''Scops''. Today the species is assigned to the genus ''Otus''. There are three subspecies currently recognised:{{cite web | url = http://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=F0AA4FE4EE5F5395 | title = African Scops Owl ''Otus senegalensis'' (Swainson, 1837) | access-date = 30 October 2016 | publisher = denis Lepage|work = Avibase}} *''O. s. senegalensis'': Widespread in sub-Saharan Africa *''O. s. nivosus'': Found in south-eastern Kenya from the lower [[Tana River (Kenya)|Tana River]] to the [[Lali Hills]] *''O. s. feae'': Endemic to [[Annobón]] island in the [[Gulf of Guinea]] (regarded by [[Birdlife International]] as a separate species, the [[Annobón scops owl]]){{cite web | url = http://www.birdlife.org/datazone/species/factsheet/61915272 | title = Annobon Scops-owl Otus feae | access-date = 30 October 2016 | publisher = Birdlife International}} The African scops owl was formerly regarded as the African [[subspecies]] of the [[Eurasian scops owl|European scops owl]]. It was grouped with the [[Arabian scops owl]], [[Socotra scops owl]], and the Annobón scops owl into ''O. senegalensis''; however, these species are now considered to be separate. ==Description== The African scops owl is a small owl, measuring {{convert|17|cm|abbr=on}} in length. It is typically greyish-brown, though sometimes pale rufous or warmer brown, and is cryptically marked with streaks and mottling. Its grey facial disk has a narrow black edge, and its eyes are yellow. It has ear tufts, which are generally kept lowered unless the bird is disturbed. The African scops owl is around {{convert|15|–|17|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length with a wingspan of {{convert|45|cm|in|abbr=on}}. ===Voice=== The African scops owl gives a monotonous quivering distinctive "prrrp" at five second intervals.{{cite book | last1 = Kemp | first1 = Alan | last2 = Kemp | first2 = Meg | year = 1998 | title = SASOL Birds of Prey of Africa and its Islands | pages = 288–289 | publisher = New Holland | ISBN = 1 85974 100 2}} ===Similar species=== The migrant [[Eurasian scops owl]] is very similar to the African scops owl; while it is typically slightly larger, it may not be distinguishable in the field.{{cite book | title = Birds of East Africa: Kenya, Tanzania, Uganda, Rwanda, Burundi | first1 = Terry | last1 = Stevenson | first2 = John | last2 = Fanshawe | year = 2002 | location = London, UK | publisher = TD & A Poyser | page = 200 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=jcN1OCKC-bQC&pg=PA20 | isbn = 0-85661-079-8}} ==Range and habitat== The African scops owl is endemic to sub-Saharan Africa. It ranges from sea level to {{convert|2000|m|abbr=on}} in elevation, and is found in wooded habitats and forest edge, including in gardens and [[mangrove]]s.{{cite book | title = A Field Guide to Birds of The Gambia and Senegal | first1 = Clive | last1 = Barlow | first2 = Tim | last2 = Wacher | year = 1997 | publisher = Yale University Press | location = New Haven, CT, US | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=1vHyDfF3vDUC&pg=PA236 | isbn = 0-300-11574-1 | page = 236}} ==Behaviour== The African scops owl is strictly nocturnal. During the day, it perches close to the trunk of a tree. When roosting in daylight, this species closes its eyes and extends its ear-tufts to give the impression of a tree branch, making it easily overlooked. Pairs sometimes roost together. The African scops owl is not as territorial as the Eurasian scops owl and will nest in loose aggregations, with the nest sites relatively close to each other.{{cite book |last1=König |first1=Claus |title=Owls A Guide to the Owls of the World |last2=Weick |first2=Friedhelm |last3=Becking |first3=Jan-Hendrick |publisher=Pica Press |year=1999 |ISBN=1-873403-74-7 |pages=231–232}} The male and female may duet, calling all night both before and after leaving the roost site. The African scops owl lays four to six eggs directly onto the floor of a [[tree hollow]], with laying occurring throughout April and June. Incubation lasts about 27 days, during which the female incubates the eggs and is fed by the male. Once the eggs hatch the young are fed by the female with food brought by the male. The young fledge in 30 days.{{cite book | title = Owls of the World | first1 = Claus | last1 = König | first2 = Friedhelm | last2 = Weick | year = 2008 | publisher = Christopher Helm | location = London, UK | page = 256 | isbn = 978-0-7136-6548-2 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=Rnz1c8olgWcC&pg=PA256}} The prey of the African scops owl is mainly insects such as [[grasshopper]]s, [[beetle]]s, [[Cricket (insect)|cricket]]s, [[moth]]s etc. but also [[spider]]s, [[scorpion]]s and small [[vertebrate]]s. They normally hunt by scanning or listening from a perch and swooping down to capture prey on the ground, but will hawk for flying insects. ==References== {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category}} * African Scops Owl - [http://sabap2.adu.org.za/docs/sabap1/396.pdf Species text in The Atlas of Southern African Birds]. * [http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/african-scops-owl-otus-senegalensis African Scops-Owl (Otus senegalensis)] videos and photos at Internet Bird Collection {{Taxonbar|from=Q1270188}} [[Category:Otus (bird)|African scops owl]] [[Category:Birds of Africa]] [[Category:Owls of Sub-Saharan Africa]] [[Category:Birds of Southern Africa]] [[Category:Birds described in 1837|African scope owl]] [[Category:Taxa named by William Swainson]]

zh.extract:
非洲角鴞（學名：Otus senegalensis，英語：African Scops Owl），是鴞形目鴟鴞科角鴞屬下的一種鳥類。這個物種分佈於撒哈拉沙漠以南的非洲，是一種體型嬌小的角鴞。目前其物種族群狀態被評為無危。

該物種由英國鳥類學家威廉·斯溫森於1837年描述。其模式產地位於甘比亞。屬名Otus來自拉丁語的同名詞，指短耳鴞，此詞源自希臘語的ὦτος（羅馬化：ōtos），意為「有耳朵的貓頭鷹」。種小名senegalensis指塞內加爾。
在生物分類學中，纵纹角鸮、歐亞角鴞、红角鸮、美洲角鸮曾被認為與這個物種形成超種的關係，但進一步的分子及叫聲證據已經顯示牠們為不同的獨立物種。而非洲角鴞原有的亞種也因此原因被獨立成種，如阿拉伯角鴞及安诺本角鸮等，但這三個物種間的關係仍未有完全解明。目前有兩個亞種，其中O. s. nivosus僅分佈於肯亞東南部。
非洲角鴞有兩種體色：灰色和棕色，耳羽較小，上半身灰色或褐色，有深色條紋和細緻的蠕蟲形紋路；肩部有白色紋路，覆羽邊緣呈紅褐色。外觀上相當類似於歐亞角鴞，但其深色斑紋較明顯，羽毛整體圖案不類似於樹皮，翅尖較圓。其眼睛虹膜黃色，鳥喙黑色，腿灰褐色，爪黑褐色。其體長約16—19公分，體重45—123公克，鳥喙寬平均約5.4公釐、深平均約9.3公釐、嘴峰長平均約17.6公釐；翼展40—45公分，翼長13.1公分；跗蹠約24.5公釐；尾長約61.3公釐。
雄鳥的歌聲為一種顫音的「kjurrrr」，每個叫聲持續約0.4—1秒，每次間隔5—8秒；雌鳥聲音則相對較高。整體音調較阿拉伯角鸮來得低。
這個物種偏好在海拔2000公尺以下，開闊乾燥、樹木眾多的稀樹草原或林地棲息，是一種留鳥。其食性與歐亞角鴞類似，以昆蟲為主，並可能在空中捕食。其繁殖季視雨季的時間有所不同，其中西非2—5月及9月，蘇丹在4—5月，東非8—12月，南非則主要在8—11月之間，採一夫一妻制，單獨繁殖。每窩通常有2—4枚卵，孵化需20—24天，約26天後離巢。
目前非洲角鴞因其極大的分佈範圍而難以估量，且在沒有證據顯示有使其族群下降的明顯威脅下，整體數量趨勢被認為是穩定的。在《國際自然保護聯盟瀕危物種紅色名錄》中，這個物種被列為無危物種。
